EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported on the campus of the University of Central Missouri in Warrensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in SE Missouri, with MLCAPE of 3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-150 m2/s2 with higher values north towards St. Louis. STP values were 2-3 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon to the east in Southern Illinois or Western Kentucky compared to Southeast Missouri. ||For Southeast Missouri, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with marginally severe wind gusts in Advance. The front reversed course and began lifting as a warm front in the morning. Though convective organization was mainly in Western Kentucky at first, late morning storms near the Missouri/Illinois border produced a brief EF-0 tornado northeast of Cape Girardeau. The mid-afternoon and late afternoon lines of storms had a glancing impact on northern portions of Southeast Missouri, with the late evening storm line finally pushing across, producing widespread wind damage in Ripley County and hail further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An enhanced area of wind in a broader storm line in Southeast Missouri produced wind gusts of 50-60 mph with pea size hail in Advance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of tennis ball size hail.

Just after sunrise, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) produced five confirmed EF-0 tornadoes. Four of these occurred in rural south-central Illinois, and another tracked through Charlack, Missouri in northern St. Louis County. No reported injuries or fatalities with these tornadoes. Also, there were a few reports of damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down 3.7 miles southeast of Lambert International Airport, on Lackland Road. As it tracked to the northeast, it snapped off several large trees and caused minor shingle, soffit and gutter damage to two houses. Another home sustained moderate siding damage. Overall, the tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 0.10 miles and a maximum width of 50 yards. The maximum sustained winds were estimated to be near 75 mph. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey confirmed a relatively long-track QLCS EF-1 tornado. This EF1 tornado began just south of Route 18 east of Passaic. Some of the worst damage of the tornado affected a farm property, where one outbuilding collapsed, another had a garage door blown off, and multiple trees were damaged or uprooted. Large debris from the damaged property was blown well northeast, in some cases over 100 yards. The tornado tracked northeast, damaging a grove of trees to the south of SH 18 and producing minor damage to an outbuilding and mobile home on the north side of the highway. The tornado then tracked northeast to Route T, producing sporadic tree damage and some minor property damage. The damage track continues east-northeast along County Road 13004, with continued sporadic tree damage. One of the larger trees fell onto a residence, damaging the property as well as to power lines. Intermittent tree damage continues northeast to Route BB and County Road 13003, with more significant tree damage eastward along Sunset Drive just west of the Henry County line. Damage becomes more intermittent eastward into Henry County, with no damage observed a half mile past the county line. However, much of this area north of 400 Road in Henry County is inaccessible, so it is possible the tornado continued a short distance longer to the north or northeast of the end of the track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a boundary over northeast Oklahoma and southeast Kansas on August 15, 2024, with thunderstorms developing just north of and along Interstate 44 by the evening. These storms persisted into the early morning hours of August 16, 2024, resulting in heavy rainfall measuring between 3.25 and 5.49 inches primarily east of Highway 65 in Webster, Wright, Howell, and Texas counties. Flash flooding occurred at multiple locations in Webster County, with areal flooding in Ozark, Douglas, and Texas counties. As the early morning hours progressed, these storms continued to push southeast out of the area by sunrise.||Later in the day, an east to west oriented frontal boundary stalled across south-central Kansas and Missouri, resulting in enough instability and deep layer shear for storms to initiate over Kansas during the early afternoon and push into southeast Kansas by the evening. As the storms moved into southwest Missouri, damaging winds up to 70 mph resulted in numerous reports of trees down in Newton, McDonald, Barry, and Taney counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of trees down on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex pushed into the Missouri Ozarks during the afternoon of July 16, 2024, resulting in showers and thunderstorms developing out ahead of it. Thunderstorm coverage and intensity increased during the overnight hours and into the early morning hours of July 17 as they developed along a stationary boundary situated west to east on the Missouri/Arkansas border. Thunderstorms first formed over northeast Oklahoma and continued to spread east into far southern Missouri, resulting in training thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Rainfall amounts between 4.0 and 8.61 inches were measured across portions of the Missouri Ozarks, with Taney County experiencing the higher end of that range. Turkey Creek in Hollister crested at 14.65 feet, causing evacuations along the creek, flooded cars and RVs, and a washed out bridge. Additionally, the first floor of City Hall in Branson was flooded, with multiple roadways flooded and damaged between Branson and Hollister in Taney County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station FW2148 in Branson measured a 12-hour rainfall total of 7.41 inches.

Additionally, the first floor of City Hall in Branson was flooded, with multiple roadways flooded and damaged between Branson and Hollister in Taney County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station FW9611 in Branson measured a 12-hour rainfall total of 6.27 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex pushed into the Missouri Ozarks during the afternoon of July 16, 2024, resulting in showers and thunderstorms developing out ahead of it. Thunderstorm coverage and intensity increased during the overnight hours and into the early morning hours of July 17 as they developed along a stationary boundary situated west to east on the Missouri/Arkansas border. Thunderstorms first formed over northeast Oklahoma and continued to spread east into far southern Missouri, resulting in training thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Rainfall amounts between 4.0 and 8.61 inches were measured across portions of the Missouri Ozarks, with Taney County experiencing the higher end of that range. Turkey Creek in Hollister crested at 14.65 feet, causing evacuations along the creek, flooded cars and RVs, and a washed out bridge. Additionally, the first floor of City Hall in Branson was flooded, with multiple roadways flooded and damaged between Branson and Hollister in Taney County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station BATM7 at Battlefield Elementary school measured a 12-hour rainfall total of 4.01 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a boundary over northeast Oklahoma and southeast Kansas on August 15, 2024, with thunderstorms developing just north of and along Interstate 44 by the evening. These storms persisted into the early morning hours of August 16, 2024, resulting in heavy rainfall measuring between 3.25 and 5.49 inches primarily east of Highway 65 in Webster, Wright, Howell, and Texas counties. Flash flooding occurred at multiple locations in Webster County, with areal flooding in Ozark, Douglas, and Texas counties. As the early morning hours progressed, these storms continued to push southeast out of the area by sunrise.||Later in the day, an east to west oriented frontal boundary stalled across south-central Kansas and Missouri, resulting in enough instability and deep layer shear for storms to initiate over Kansas during the early afternoon and push into southeast Kansas by the evening. As the storms moved into southwest Missouri, damaging winds up to 70 mph resulted in numerous reports of trees down in Newton, McDonald, Barry, and Taney counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a tree down across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front provided focus for convective initiation during the afternoon and evening of March 13th. Discrete thunderstorms grew upscale and produced up to 1.50 inch hail in portions of central and southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A discrete cell rapidly intensified over Jefferson County, producing hailstones between 1 and 1.50 inches in diameter over the course of 45 minutes in Hillsboro, Olympian Village, Crystal City, and Festus. No damage was reported. The thunderstorm kept producing severe hail after it exited into Illinois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously cold airmass moved into the Ozarks on January 13th bringing high temperatures around Zero degrees with wind chills in -20 to -25 degree range for some locations. As precipitation overspread the cold air in place over the region on the 14th, accumulating snow fell across much of southern Missouri. Multiple snow amounts from 1 to 4 inches occurred across the Ozarks. Roads became snow covered to partially covered and slippery. Schools were closed due to winter weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass slid into the Ozarks during the overnight hours of January 13-14 bringing extreme cold and windchills in the -20 to -25 degree range across most of the  region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures were observed in northern portions of southeast Missouri during the early morning hours on the 6th. The lowest temperature recorded was 30 degrees in Perry and Wayne counties. Temperatures were highly variable near the freezing mark across the rest of the region, but surface conditions were still favorable for frost formation. An area of high pressure allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Any unprotected crops, vegetation, shrubs, and trees were late enough in the growing season to be impacted by the freezing temperatures and frost. Communities had also began turning on outdoor sprinkler irrigation systems for the warm season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large mid latitude extratropical cyclone moved across the central US on Jan 8th into Jan 9th, producing a large swath of 5+ inch snow across Missouri. Snow fell most of the day on Jan 8th, but with surface temperatures above freezing accumulations were marginal. The heaviest snow fell in the early morning hours of Jan 9th just to the west of the cyclone dry slot, and with surface temperatures falling into the 20s, snow accumulated very quickly. Snow came to an end from west to east on the afternoon of Jan 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grundy County EM reported 12 inches of snow near South Lineville in Mercer County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grundy County EM reported 12 inches of snow near South Lineville in Mercer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation deficits over the previous several months had several counties across central MO and northeastern MO in severe to extreme drought at the start of January 2024. Widespread snowfall helped these counties improve to only moderate drought (D1) or better by the January 16th issuance of the US Drought Monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pettis County began January 2024 within severe drought (D2). Widespread snowfall helped Pettis County improve to moderate drought (D1) by the January 16th issuance of the US Drought Monitor, with additional precipitation improving Pettis County to D0 (abnormally dry) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State. ||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-2500 J/kg in SE Missouri. Effective bulk shear was 40-50 kts with STP of 1-3. Precipitable water was 1.6-1.9 inches, allowing for torrential rainfall rates.||The morning round of storms produced widespread straight-line wind damage across the southernmost portions of SE Missouri, especially Ripley to Stoddard Counties. Three strong tornadoes occurred during the early morning in SE Missouri. The first was a long-track EF-2 tornado beginning in Oregon County, then crossed the entirety of Ripley County before lifting on the SW side of Poplar Bluff. A destructive downburst in Dexter transitioned into an EF-2 tornado that tracked northeastward to southeast of Morley in Scott County. An EF-3 tornado that began in far eastern Stoddard County moved northeastward through Sikeston to Diehlstadt with widespread damage in Sikeston. One person (43m, indirect) in Southeast Sikeston lost his life due to a falling tree branch during cleanup. Another person (51f, indirect) in Morehouse died as a result of an unattended candle, used for lighting during the resulting power outage, causing a house fire. Flash flooding occurred in several counties in SE Missouri as a result of high rain rates with the first round of storms, with late morning storms extending flood duration. A majority of Ripley, Butler, Stoddard, and New Madrid Counties were without power as of late morning, with significant outages also occurring in Wayne, Scott, and Mississippi Counties.||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 3500-4500 J/kg in SE Missouri. For SE Missouri, effective bulk shear rose to 45-55 kts, with SRH around 200-250 m2/s2 and STP of 2-4. 0-1 km shear was 15-20 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1200-1500 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds.||Late afternoon to early evening supercells were mainly in S Illinois and W Kentucky. The few in SE Missouri approached far western counties. After several chaser videos of tornadoes west of Carter County, the cold front caught up to the storm and Carter County primarily had straight-line wind damage as a result, with a brief EF-0 tornado. Another tornado, an EF-1, entered Bollinger County southeast of Marquand. Several straight-line wind reports were received, along with more flash flooding as lingering high water and saturated soils from the day were quickly reinvigorated. 2-5 inches of rain fell across the southern two rows of counties, with some of the highest rainfall totals near the Black River and south of Cape Girardeau. More power outages occurred with Carter and Bollinger County outages, along with the seven counties from the morning with substantial outage numbers, continuing through the night. The Black River briefly flooded in Poplar Bluff following this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was blown down from thunderstorm winds. The tree limb struck a man in the back, who died as a result (43m, direct).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures were observed in portions of southeast Missouri during the early morning hours on the 22nd. The lowest temperature recorded was 29 degrees in Wayne county. While temperatures were highly variable with many locations remaining above freezing, surface conditions were still favorable for localized frost formation. High pressure had moved over the region that night and allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Any unprotected crops, vegetation, shrubs, and trees were late enough in the growing season to be impacted by the freezing temperatures and frost. Impacts were fairly isolated and minimal due to the short duration.

During the night on May 6th, 2024, a line of severe thunderstorms moved across western Missouri and impacted the NWS St. Louis forecast area during the early morning of May 7th. While damaging straight-line wind was observed across parts of the region, three QLCS tornadoes were detected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs were reported to be down across town in Madison. A power pole was also snapped here. A carport was blown out along Route A, but the exact location was unknown. Locations within the path of this storm include Madison and Holliday.

While damaging straight-line wind was observed across parts of the region, three QLCS tornadoes were detected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees were reported to be downed due to thunderstorm winds. This includes multiple trees snapped along the upper portion of the trunk and uprooted near Cuba, a tree over the roadway near Roadside Park, a tree over the roadway at the 1200 block of Highway 19, a tree over the roadway along Highway 49 near Dan Halbert Road near Cherryville, and two trees down in Bourbon: one over the roadway at Highway C near Gerth Road and another over the roadway at the 1000 and 1500 blocks of Old Springfield Road. Additional cities in the path of this storm were Steelville and Leasburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours of May 27th, a few severe storms moved through Iowa, Illinois, and northeast Missouri. While there were not many storms, they remained strong to severe for most of the entire path through the area, producing large hail reports for the most part, along with some damaging winds, and one isolated EF0 tornado. Most notably, these storms produced significant swaths of small hail, accumulating deep enough to affect roads and leave piles near homes that lasted hours. Some areas of crops were damaged by the small hail and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage to sheds, houses and trees also relayed.

The tornado did extensive and severe tree damage through the south side of Sikeston. Approaching and crossing Malone Ave, the tornado removed part of the roof of a school building and a nearby former farm supply store, where cinder block walls also collapsed. North of Malone Ave, the tornado collapsed a tall wooden light pole at a ball field, and removed most of the roof of a house. The tornado turned more northeast as it exited Sikeston, doing damage to trees, power lines, and outbuildings before ending in the community of Diehlstadt. Peak winds for this second Scott County portion were 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface low pressure was moving through Kansas towards Iowa with a cold front stretching southward. Strong wind gusts of 40-55 mph were observed at several sites through the day in Southeast Missouri with the strongest measured gust 54 mph at the Cape Girardeau ASOS. Multiple trees were blown down onto roads with one across Highway T near Doniphan in Ripley County, one on Hwy 34 outside Marble Hill in Bollinger County, and one on Bloomfield Rd in Cape Girardeau County. Multiple wind damage reports came in from Stoddard County, with a stable crushed by a tree in Dexter, a tree falling onto a home in Bloomfield, and shingle damage at another home in Bloomfield. In New Madrid County, a roof was blown off a barn a few miles SSE of Canalou and, in Portageville, 2 trees were downed and an outbuilding collapsed. Areas of blowing dust were observed during the afternoon in the flatlands, causing visibility issues on roads. Wind speeds decreased but remained breezy in the late afternoon as showers moved into SE Missouri, with a boundary moving through during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortly after sunset, while decent instability (CAPE of 1000-1500 J/kg) remained, a line of SW to NE oriented storms developed in SE Missouri from Butler to Bollinger counties. A couple of these storms produced hail, with the most prolific hail producer resulting in numerous reports of 1-1.75 inch hail on the south side of Poplar Bluff. These storms were likely amplified by a small-scale wave channeling vorticity into SEMO. With shear around 20 kts and lapse rates of 5-6 C/km, severe weather potential dropped off for the late evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail occurred along Highway 51.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hail observations were reported from Poplar Bluff including 1.5 inch hail near the Industrial Park on MO-142, golf ball and half-dollar size hail on the south side of Poplar Bluff, and hail estimated at 1.5 inch damaging a windshield at CR 605 and Hwy 53.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface low pressure was moving through Kansas towards Iowa with a cold front stretching southward. Strong wind gusts of 40-55 mph were observed at several sites through the day in Southeast Missouri with the strongest measured gust 54 mph at the Cape Girardeau ASOS. Multiple trees were blown down onto roads with one across Highway T near Doniphan in Ripley County, one on Hwy 34 outside Marble Hill in Bollinger County, and one on Bloomfield Rd in Cape Girardeau County. Multiple wind damage reports came in from Stoddard County, with a stable crushed by a tree in Dexter, a tree falling onto a home in Bloomfield, and shingle damage at another home in Bloomfield. In New Madrid County, a roof was blown off a barn a few miles SSE of Canalou and, in Portageville, 2 trees were downed and an outbuilding collapsed. Areas of blowing dust were observed during the afternoon in the flatlands, causing visibility issues on roads. Wind speeds decreased but remained breezy in the late afternoon as showers moved into SE Missouri, with a boundary moving through during the evening.

Thankfully, there were no reported injuries or fatalities with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on East Lane, about 2.4 miles southwest of the I-270/I-55 interchange in southern St. Louis County and tracked to the southeast. It snapped off numerous tree limbs, uprooted several large trees and caused minor damage to roofs and siding on a couple of houses. However, one house sustained moderate damage when the roof over the carport was lifted off and dropped just to the left of the house. The tornado then crossed I-55. Between I-55 and the Mississippi River, just numerous snapped tree limbs and uprooted trees were noted as it moved through Oakville. Then the tornado crossed the Mississippi River into Monroe County, Illinois. The tornado was rated EF1 in St. Louis County with a path length of 5.34 miles and a max path width of 172 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, which stretched from Oklahoma to Central Illinois over to Northern Ohio, progressed slowly through the Quad State region, with an MCS moving through during the evening hours. An early afternoon SGF sounding placed MLCAPE at around 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 50-55 kts with 0-1 km shear of 30 kts with 200 m2/s2 SRH. In Southeast Missouri, several wind damage reports were received with trees down in several locations, and a roof blown off a house in Vanduser. A 66 mph wind gust was observed at the Cape Girardeau ASOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down near Puxico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night on May 6th, 2024, a line of severe thunderstorms moved across western Missouri and impacted the NWS St. Louis forecast area during the early morning of May 7th. While damaging straight-line wind was observed across parts of the region, three QLCS tornadoes were detected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just south of Indian Ridge Lane, 2.9 miles northwest of Defiance. As it tracked to the east northeast it snapped off or uprooted several large trees, as well as snapping off numerous tree limbs. No structural damage was reported. The tornado lifted and dissipated around 3 miles north of Defiance.  Overall, the tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 2.23 miles and a max path width of 50 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures were observed in portions of southeast Missouri during the early morning hours on the 22nd. The lowest temperature recorded was 29 degrees in Wayne county. While temperatures were highly variable with many locations remaining above freezing, surface conditions were still favorable for localized frost formation. High pressure had moved over the region that night and allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Any unprotected crops, vegetation, shrubs, and trees were late enough in the growing season to be impacted by the freezing temperatures and frost. Impacts were fairly isolated and minimal due to the short duration.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited amounts of rainfall across portions of central Missouri allowed portions of the region to experience Severe (D2) Drought during the first half of the month. The counties of Maries, Phelps and Dent were initially impacted the first week of March, However, by the end of the month, ample enough rainfall ad occurred to alleviate Severe (D2) Drought conditions for all but extreme eastern Dent County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall amounts during the first half of the month lead to Severe (D2) Drought development. Rainfall returned towards the end of the month allowing for some improvements, though portions of extreme Dent County remained in D2 drought at the end of the month. Little if any impacts to agriculture occurred tough minor impacts to soil moisture, ground water and surface (streams) water were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swath of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall fell from March 7th to March 8th to the northwest of Springfield. Minor flooding of low water crossings occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several low water crossings around Collins were flooded and impassable. Coon Creek and Brush Creek were both out of their banks in these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms moved through southern Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours on the 14th. A swath of damaging winds occurred along with intermittent large hail and one weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tractor trailer was blown over along Highway 160 and Spokane road. The roadway was blocked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The bi-state region was threatened with severe thunderstorms on May 8th. Numerous supercells produced very large hail most of the day, and a small QLCS produced multiple tornadoes in southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources including the Department of Highways, law enforcement, and emergency management reported flooding in and around Sullivan, Stanton, Gerald, and Piney Park. Reports include water running over over roadways and deep pooling in yards in Sullivan and in the Melody Lakes subdivision near Gerald. Water was reported across east and westbound I-44 at mile marker 230. Missouri State Highway Patrol closed Highway 185 at East Vine in Sullivan due to a water rescue. They also closed Highway K and Chapel Hill Road near Piney Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening of May 26, 2024, ten tornadoes touched down in the St. Louis metropolitan area and southwest Illinois from a mix of supercells and QLCS modes. This occurred alongside numerous reports of large hail of up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging winds of up to 81 mph. Thankfully, there were no reported injuries or fatalities with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county official and fire department/rescue reported up to 1.5 inch diameter hailstones in  Bridgeton and Hazelwood. Hailstones between 1 and 1.5 inches fell as far east as Florissant, Berkeley, and Ferguson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures were observed in portions of southeast Missouri during the early morning hours on the 22nd. The lowest temperature recorded was 29 degrees in Wayne county. While temperatures were highly variable with many locations remaining above freezing, surface conditions were still favorable for localized frost formation. High pressure had moved over the region that night and allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Any unprotected crops, vegetation, shrubs, and trees were late enough in the growing season to be impacted by the freezing temperatures and frost. Impacts were fairly isolated and minimal due to the short duration.

The bi-state region was threatened with severe thunderstorms on May 8th. Numerous supercells produced very large hail most of the day, and a small QLCS produced multiple tornadoes in southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on Jakes Prairie Road, about 6.5 miles northwest of Cuba. It uprooted and snapped off several large trees, then it tracked to the east. As it approached Four Mile Road it either uprooted or snapped off several more large trees before lifting and dissipating on Pleasant Valley Road, just west of intersection with Highway 19. Overall, the tornado was rated EF1 with a path length of 2.24 miles and a max path width of 75 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The bi-state region was threatened with severe thunderstorms on May 8th. Numerous supercells produced very large hail most of the day, and a small QLCS produced multiple tornadoes in southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the tornado moved into Madison County it began to move in a more south southeasterly direction, crossing over Highway 72 once again. It snapped off or uprooted numerous large trees throughout this portion of the tornado. Around 2.1 miles southeast of Roselle, it crossed over Highway 72 one final time and remained to the north of Highway 72 for the remainder of it���s path. The tornado lifted and dissipated 6.6 miles east southeast of Roselle. In Madison County, the tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 6.64 miles and a max path width of 75 yards. Overall, the tornado was rated EF1 with a path length of 12.01 miles and a max path width of 150 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down on MO-32 north of Belleview Schools.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms congealed into a QLCS ahead of a cold front on May 21st. As the line matured, thunderstorm wind gusts produced damage in east-central Missouri. Much further to the north and south, discrete thunderstorms caused wind and hail damage in Knox County and Iron and Madison Counties, respectively.

A cluster of severe thunderstorms moved through southern Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours on the 14th. A swath of damaging winds occurred along with intermittent large hail and one weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One home completely lost its roof however no windows or glass was broken at the home. Another home lost significant portions of its roof and the garage door was bent inward. A large tree was also uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms moved through southern Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours on the 14th. A swath of damaging winds occurred along with intermittent large hail and one weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home on State Highway F had significant roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms moved through southern Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours on the 14th. A swath of damaging winds occurred along with intermittent large hail and one weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes had shingle damage and there were small trees down in a south Ozark subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms moved through southern Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours on the 14th. A swath of damaging winds occurred along with intermittent large hail and one weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent tornado path with EF-0 damage occurred just northeast of Ozark. The tornado traveled one mile with a maximum width of 50 yards. Multiple trees were uprooted or snapped along the path. The tornado also damaged a barn and outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours, storms brough large hail and damaging winds to much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported in the western portions of the Crossroads south of downtown Kansas City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of precipitation overspread a below freezing airmass over southeastern Kansas. The combination of cold temperatures and precipitation produced widespread reports from a glaze of ice to a quarter of an inch accumulation in south central Missouri.  Roads were impacted and schools closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broad area of precipitation overspread a below freezing airmass over the Missouri Ozarks. The combination of cold temperatures and precipitation produced widespread reports of a glaze to a tenth of an inch of ice. Roads were impacted and schools closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously cold airmass moved into the Ozarks on January 13th bringing high temperatures around Zero degrees with wind chills in -20 to -25 degree range for some locations. As precipitation overspread the cold air in place over the region on the 14th, accumulating snow fell across much of southern Missouri. Multiple snow amounts from 1 to 4 inches occurred across the Ozarks. Roads became snow covered to partially covered and slippery. Schools were closed due to winter weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An anomalously cold airmass moved into the Ozarks on January 13th bringing high temperatures around Zero degrees with wind chills in -20 to -30 degree range for some locations. As precipitation overspread the cold air in place over the region on the 14th, accumulating snow fell across much of southern Missouri. Multiple snow amounts from 1 to 4 inches occurred across the County. Several social media posts from the public reported measured storm totals of 4 inches of snow near Goodman, 3.3 and 2.9 inches near Anderson and other reports in excess of an inch. Roads became snow covered and slippery. Schools were closed due to winter weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved northeast out of northwestern Arkansas and pushed across southern Missouri. This line of strong to severe storms interacted with a weak boundary which allowed storms to produce hail from penny to quarters in size and isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold surface temperatures and light rainfall occurring behind a storm system that brough localized hail produces isolated freezing rainfall. Temperatures fell rapidly into the 20s causing a flash freeze. From a glaze to a tenth of an inch off ice was measured. A few slide offs occurred from icy roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation falling as surface temperatures fell below the freezing mark allowed from a glaze to up to a tenth inch of flat ice to occur. The NWS ASOS at the Springfield Branson National Airport measured 0.08 inches of flat ice accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rolla/Vichy ASOS reported a flat ice amount of 0.01 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy wet snow and gusty north winds to eastern IA, northwest IL, and northeast MO on January 9. Snowfall amounts between 6 and 10 inches were common, with higher totals of 11 to 15 inches falling mainly in Dubuque, Jones, Linn, and Johnson counties in Iowa.  Several accidents occurred on area roads, with a long backup/delay observed on Interstate 80 Tuesday that evening near Iowa City, IA. In addition, strong north winds over 30 mph were common with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow on January 8 and 9, with between 8 to 10 inches common throughout the county. The local COOP Observer in Memphis measured a storm total snow of 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog of a quarter mile or less visibility redeveloped in Southeast Missouri during the early morning hours of the 24th as a result of calm winds, light drizzle, and dew points in the mid 40s to the lower 50s, which drew in moisture from frozen near-surface soils. Heavier rainfall had occurred in the afternoon to evening on the 23rd. Dense fog continued throughout the remainder of the morning hours. On a broader scale, a large-scale fog event was developing, stretching from the Upper Midwest to the Southern Plains.

A cold airmass moved into the region as precipitation moved over the area. Precipitation started as a light rain/snow mixture during the early morning of January 9 before changing over to snow just before sunrise. Snow then continued to fall into the afternoon hours before tapering off. Snow amounts from 1 to 3 inches fell across portions of southwestern and central Missouri. Roads became snow covered to partially covered and slippery. Schools were closed due to winter weather where snowfall occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation streamed east-northeastward across the Tennessee River Valley on the 14th and 15th. The northern fringes of this system crossed the Quad State in two primary waves, one on the 14th during the afternoon into the early evening, and the second on the 15th during the morning. The first wave on the 14th resulted in 1-2'' of snowfall in much of SEMO while, further north, snowfall amounts were less than an inch. The second wave on the 15th resulted in an additional inch of snow in far Southeast Missouri.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Improvement in drought conditions across the Ozarks occurred through the month. Severe (D2) drought contracted in parts of central and southern Missouri. Locations in central Missouri saw improvement from Extreme (D3) drought at the beginning of the month to Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions by the end of January. Some locations saw the removal of all drought conditions. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers continued. Limited impacts to crops occurred as the growing season generally begins in late March to April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Enough rain fell towards the middle to end of the month to improve conditions back to Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions. Farm and stock ponds continued to see impacts along with ground water levels and reservoirs. Crop loss estimates notated are for limited loses this month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Improvement in drought conditions across the Ozarks occurred through the month. Severe (D2) drought contracted in parts of central and southern Missouri. Locations in central Missouri saw improvement from Extreme (D3) drought at the beginning of the month to Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions by the end of January. Some locations saw the removal of all drought conditions. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers continued. Limited impacts to crops occurred as the growing season generally begins in late March to April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Enough rain fell towards the middle to end of the month to improve conditions back to Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions. Farm and stock ponds continued to see impacts along with ground water levels and reservoirs. Crop loss estimates notated are for limited loses this month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With surface low pressure well to the southeast and continued northwesterly flow of Arctic air, clear skies and fresh snow cover from the 1-3'' of snow that fell the 14th-15th in southern portions of SEMO allowed for frigid temperatures and wind chill values. Lows dropped to 0-5 degrees across SEMO. Minimum wind chills ranged from -4 in Sikeston to -15 in Perryville.

Dry deep soil moisture and slightly below normal precipitation in December resulted in temporary D2 drought conditions in northern portions of Southeast Missouri lingering into early January. With the agricultural season over, there was very little impact. Multiple moderate to heavy rainfall events the second week of January brought an end to D2 drought by the middle of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation streamed northeastward across the Quad State region on the 22nd. An extended period of cold weather had left surface conditions frigid while warm air advection aloft advanced into the area. However, sufficient warm advection at the surface warmed up temperatures in most of SEMO to keep precipitation only rain. Carter, Bollinger, and Perry counties had multiple hours in the morning of light freezing rain, which produced hazardous road conditions, leading to multiple slide-offs in and around Ellsinore. By late morning, temperatures had warmed above freezing, precipitation switched to rain, and conditions improved.

A broad area of high pressure moved over the Quad State region and surrounding areas as a cold front pushed off the Gulf and Atlantic coasts. Northwesterly winds of 5-10 mph remained in the early morning hours on the 20th. Lows in SEMO early on the 20th dropped to 4-9 degrees with wind chills generally -5 to -15. The lowest wind chill was in Perry County.

Above normal rainfall in much of Missouri, Illinois, and Southeast Iowa for April led to elevated river levels by the end of the month on the Mississippi River. The flood stage at Chester was exceeded just before the end of the month, continuing into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation streamed east-northeastward across the Tennessee River Valley on the 14th and 15th. The northern fringes of this system crossed the Quad State in two primary waves, one on the 14th during the afternoon into the early evening, and the second on the 15th during the morning. The first wave on the 14th resulted in 1-2'' of snowfall in much of SEMO while, further north, snowfall amounts were less than an inch. The second wave on the 15th resulted in an additional inch of snow in far Southeast Missouri.

An Arctic front moved through the Quad State region in the evening hours of the 18th through the early AM hours of the 19th. Ahead of the front, overrunning precipitation early morning on the 18th produced a wintry mix in the far southeastern Missouri counties and along the KY border with TN. Amounts in far SE Missouri were light and skewed towards reports of sleet. Additional very light mixed precipitation occurred in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass moved into the region as precipitation moved over the area. Precipitation started as a light rain/snow mixture during the early morning of January 9 before changing over to snow just before sunrise. Snow then continued to fall into the afternoon hours before tapering off. Snow amounts from 1 to 3 inches fell across portions of southwestern and central Missouri. Roads became snow covered to partially covered and slippery. Schools were closed due to winter weather where snowfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as a light rain/snow mixture during the early morning of January 9 before changing over to snow just before sunrise. Snow then continued to fall into the afternoon hours before tapering off. Snow amounts around an inch fell across the county. A Co-Op Observer report of a measured storm total of 0.5 inch of snow was received from north of Lamar. Roads became snow covered to partially covered and slippery. Schools were closed due to winter weather.

Roads became snow covered and slippery with several slide offs reported. Schools were closed due to winter weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long duration fog event continued from mid afternoon of the 24th through the morning of the 25th. Dense fog formation of a quarter mile or less was most widespread in Southeast Missouri during the evening on the 24th and the early morning on the 25th. Calm winds, light drizzle, and dew points in the lower 50s over a rapidly diminishing soil frost depth contributed to fog formation. This fog event had the largest coverage area in over a decade, with Dense Fog Advisories stretching from PA to ND to TX to portions of the FL Panhandle.

Improvement in drought conditions across the Ozarks occurred through the month. Severe (D2) drought contracted in parts of central and southern Missouri. Locations in central Missouri saw improvement from Extreme (D3) drought at the beginning of the month to Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions by the end of January. Some locations saw the removal of all drought conditions. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers continued. Limited impacts to crops occurred as the growing season generally begins in late March to April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Enough rain fell towards the middle to end of the month to improve conditions back to Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. Farm and stock ponds continued to see impacts along with ground water levels and reservoirs. Crop loss estimates notated are for limited loses this month though drought conditions continue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of stratiform rainfall on the 26th continuing into the morning of the 27th produced rain amounts from 1 to 1.5 inches across portions of southern Missouri. This allows local streams to rise out of their banks impacting a few low water crossings and roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway J at Mincey Creek was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong surface low, which set a January record for lowest pressure observed at Evansville, IN, advanced northeastward from St Louis towards Chicago during the day on the 9th. A cold front pushed through the Quad State with gusty winds ahead of the front from the south and behind the front from the west. Winds were gusty from the early afternoon through the evening, with the strongest gusts around 35-45 mph generally occurring during the mid-afternoon hours. The strongest measured gust in SEMO was 45 mph at the Cape Girardeau Regional Airport ASOS.

Precipitation deficits over the previous several months had several counties across central MO and northeastern MO in severe to extreme drought at the start of January 2024. Widespread snowfall helped these counties improve to only moderate drought (D1) or better by the January 16th issuance of the US Drought Monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooper County began January 2024 within severe drought (D2). Widespread snowfall helped Cooper County improve to moderate drought (D1) by the January 16th issuance of the US Drought Monitor, with additional precipitation improving Cooper County to D0 (abnormally dry) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold temperatures and wind chills arrived by the evening of January 12th, and continued through January 16th. This cold air outbreak brought the wind chills since 1989 in many locations, including Kansas City. After a brief warmup, extreme cold returned for Jan 19th through Jan 21st. Wind chills were below zero for as long as 5 days (120 hours) in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high and low temperatures at Kirksville airport were as follows:||Jan 12: High: 29  Low: 10|Jan 13: High: 10  Low:  -13|Jan 14: High: -6   Low: -17|Jan 15: High: 0    Low: -17|Jan 16: High: 6    Low: -9||Jan 19: High: 9   Low: -5|Jan 20: High: 11  Low: -13|Jan 21: High: 30  Low: -6||The coldest wind chill recorded during this cold air outbreak was -40 degrees at 4 AM on January 14th. This was the coldest wind chill recorded at Kirksville since the morning of December 22, 1989 (-43 degrees).||The 4 day period of Jan 13-16 was the coldest 4 day period on record in Kirksville, with an average temperature of -5.7 degrees. (Period of record begins Jan 1, 1948).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system began digging southeast across the southern Plains during the morning of February 12, with a surface low located across Louisiana. As the surface low tracked to the east and northeast throughout the day, several bands of snow developed northwest of the low across the Missouri Ozarks. Precipitation began as light rain during the early morning hours before transitioning completely to snow by mid morning. Precipitation then came to an end during the afternoon as the system pushed out of the Missouri Ozarks.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches across southwest Missouri and south central Missouri to as little as 0.2 inches towards central Missouri. Roads in far southwest Missouri became mostly to completely snow covered during the morning, with partly covered roadways farther northeast into the Springfield metropolitan area into late morning. Several schools scattered throughout the Missouri Ozarks were closed for the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as a light rain and snow mixture during the early morning of February 12 before changing over to snow later in the morning, with the snow then continuing into the late afternoon hours. A Co-op observer reported a measured storm total of 2.0 inches of snow in Mansfield. Roads became snow covered and slippery. Numerous schools were closed on Monday, February 12.

Precipitation began as light rain during the early morning hours before transitioning completely to snow by mid morning. Precipitation then came to an end during the afternoon as the system pushed out of the Missouri Ozarks.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches across southwest Missouri and south central Missouri to as little as 0.2 inches towards central Missouri. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as a light rain and snow mixture during the early morning of February 12 before changing over to snow later in the morning, with the snow then continuing into the afternoon hours. Snow amounts between 2.0 and 4.7 inches fell across the county. A public report of a measured storm total of 4.5 inches of snow was received 5 miles northeast of Phillipsburg, with a measured 4.7 inches 1 mile north of Lebanon. Roads became snow covered and slippery. Numerous schools were closed on Monday, February 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite mostly mild conditions this month, a cold front moved through and brought a brief period of colder weather and snow to the region. Snow began on the morning of the 16th and became heavy through the early afternoon hours. Snowfall rates exceeded 1 inch per hour, helping to overcome warm ground temperatures and lead to hazardous driving conditions. There were some reports of thundersnow as well, an indication of rare atmospheric instability and particularly intense snowfall. A total of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell in a band near Interstate 70. Numerous traffic accidents were reported during the heart of the storm, some which closed down major interstate highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of heavy snow brought 4 to 6 inches of snowfall. Snowfall rates exceeded 1 inch per hour and led to hazardous driving conditions. Numerous vehicle accidents were reported.

Numerous traffic accidents were reported during the heart of the storm, some which closed down major interstate highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of heavy snow brought 4 to 6 inches of snowfall. Snowfall rates exceeded 1 inch per hour and led to hazardous driving conditions. Numerous vehicle accidents were reported including on area interstate highways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system began digging southeast across the southern Plains during the morning of February 12, with a surface low located across Louisiana. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 1 pm on February 8th, a power line was downed along County Road 434 near the intersection of County Road 427 in southeast Howard County due to 45 to 50 mph WSW wind gusts. With 45-50 WSW wind gusts, RH around 30 percent, and near record warm temperatures, the fire spread rapidly, and ultimately ended up burning around 80 acres of grass, brush, and trees. The fire was contained and finally completely extinguished by around 445 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 1 pm on February 8th, a power line was downed along County Road 434 near the intersection of County Road 427 in southeast Howard County due to 40 to 45 mph SW wind gusts. With 40-45 WSW wind gusts, RH around 30 percent, and near record warm temperatures, the fire spread rapidly, and ultimately ended up burning around 80 acres of grass, brush, and trees. The fire was contained and finally completely extinguished by around 445 pm. No structures were damaged or destroyed and there were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage made its way through the Quad State region during the afternoon on the 22nd. MUCAPE was limited to around 500 J/kg with 0-3 km helicity at 100-200 m2/s2. The frontal boundary became a focus for a line of thunderstorms, with mid-level lapse rates and shear robust enough to produce hail, with marginally severe hail reported in Bollinger and Cape Girardeau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail came from a storm embedded in a line moving southeastward through SE Missouri.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through SE Missouri around 0000-0300CST on Feb 28th as strengthening low pressure moved towards the Great Lakes. Gusty gradient winds occurred ahead of the front during the afternoon to evening hours and behind the front towards early morning. Thunderstorm activity in SE Missouri overnight was most organized with the frontal passage in far southeastern portions of the state. The strongest wind gust observation was 48 mph at Cape Girardeau ASOS during the afternoon on the 27th.

Numerous schools were closed on Monday, February 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system began digging southeast across the southern Plains during the morning of February 12, with a surface low located across Louisiana. As the surface low tracked to the east and northeast throughout the day, several bands of snow developed northwest of the low across the Missouri Ozarks. Precipitation began as light rain during the early morning hours before transitioning completely to snow by mid morning. Precipitation then came to an end during the afternoon as the system pushed out of the Missouri Ozarks.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches across southwest Missouri and south central Missouri to as little as 0.2 inches towards central Missouri. Roads in far southwest Missouri became mostly to completely snow covered during the morning, with partly covered roadways farther northeast into the Springfield metropolitan area into late morning. Several schools scattered throughout the Missouri Ozarks were closed for the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as a light rain and snow mixture during the early morning of February 12 before changing over to snow later in the morning, with the snow then continuing into the late afternoon hours. Snow amounts between 2.5 and 5 inches fell across the county. A public report of a measured storm total of 3.5 inches of snow was received 3 miles south of Mansfield, with a measured 5.0 inches of snow in Ava. Roads became snow covered and slippery, and numerous schools were closed on Monday, February 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred on May 8, 2024. The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal April rainfall in much of Missouri, Illinois, and Southeast Iowa led to elevated river levels early May on the Mississippi River. The flood stage at Chester was exceeded just before the beginning of April, while Cape Girardeau and Thebes reached minor flood in early May, albeit for a short duration at Thebes. Rainy weather May 5th-8th and 14th-17th extended minor flood conditions at Chester and Cape Girardeau through the first three weeks of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding began near Chester at the end of April and continued through the first three weeks of May with impacts limited to low-lying agricultural areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure from a mid-Janaury cold snap remained anchored in place across the Mid-South. A weak mid-level shortwave moved into the Mid-South on January 18, 2024, as a weak surface low-pressure system tracked from the Red River Valley across southern Arkansas. As a result, overrunning developed across the area during the early morning hours. Temperatures were below freezing and light freezing rain/freezing drizzle fell across the area resulting in slick roads and numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice accumulated on roadways and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the morning and afternoon hours of April 16th, a strong low pressure system brought severe weather to much of Missouri. Most of the storm reports were from damaging straight line winds, but there was large hail across NE Missouri and two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey confirmed an EF-1 tornado at Smithville Lake in northwestern Clay County on the morning of April 16th. This tornado was associated with a mesovortex within a squall line. The tornado did most of its damage near Camp Branch Dr and Collins Road where it flipped a trailer and badly damaged a building owned by the Kansas City Trapshooters Association. The tornado ended near County Road W just east of the community of Paradise. In total, the tornado traveled about 2.3 miles, was 50 yards wide, and was rated as an EF-1 with peak winds of 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening on May 26, 2024. Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mping report of ping pong ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front pushed through the Missouri Ozarks during the late evening of May 6, 2024 and into the early morning hours of May 7, 2024. Thunderstorms developed along the front west of the Ozarks, quickly growing upscale to a linear storm mode by the time they reached southwest Missouri. This line of thunderstorms pushed through the area, causing widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of several uprooted trees and snapped branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region during the afternoon and evening hours of March 14th. There were widespread reports of large hail as the storms moved through, some up to 3 inches in diameter. There were also several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of very large hail fell along the I-70 corridor in St. Charles County. The largest hail stones were around three inches in diameter. The cities that the large hail affected were Wentzville, Saint Paul, O'Fallon, Lake St. Louis, Flint Hill, Saint Peters, Cottleville, and Saint Charles. Numerous vehicles sustained hail damage including thousands of individual cars and at 12 car dealerships. One dealership alone had 450 cars damaged from the large hail. Also, hundreds of homes and businesses sustained roof and siding damage. One school sustained damage to skylights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell on the north side of Senath.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal April rainfall in much of Missouri, Illinois, and Southeast Iowa led to elevated river levels early May on the Mississippi River. The flood stage at Chester was exceeded just before the beginning of April, while Cape Girardeau and Thebes reached minor flood in early May, albeit for a short duration at Thebes. Rainy weather May 5th-8th and 14th-17th extended minor flood conditions at Chester and Cape Girardeau through the first three weeks of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding briefly occurred near Thebes in early May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State. ||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-2500 J/kg in SE Missouri. Effective bulk shear was 40-50 kts with STP of 1-3. Precipitable water was 1.6-1.9 inches, allowing for torrential rainfall rates.||The morning round of storms produced widespread straight-line wind damage across the southernmost portions of SE Missouri, especially Ripley to Stoddard Counties. Three strong tornadoes occurred during the early morning in SE Missouri. The first was a long-track EF-2 tornado beginning in Oregon County, then crossed the entirety of Ripley County before lifting on the SW side of Poplar Bluff. A destructive downburst in Dexter transitioned into an EF-2 tornado that tracked northeastward to southeast of Morley in Scott County. An EF-3 tornado that began in far eastern Stoddard County moved northeastward through Sikeston to Diehlstadt with widespread damage in Sikeston. One person (43m, indirect) in Southeast Sikeston lost his life due to a falling tree branch during cleanup. Another person (51f, indirect) in Morehouse died as a result of an unattended candle, used for lighting during the resulting power outage, causing a house fire. Flash flooding occurred in several counties in SE Missouri as a result of high rain rates with the first round of storms, with late morning storms extending flood duration. A majority of Ripley, Butler, Stoddard, and New Madrid Counties were without power as of late morning, with significant outages also occurring in Wayne, Scott, and Mississippi Counties.||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 3500-4500 J/kg in SE Missouri. For SE Missouri, effective bulk shear rose to 45-55 kts, with SRH around 200-250 m2/s2 and STP of 2-4. 0-1 km shear was 15-20 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1200-1500 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds.||Late afternoon to early evening supercells were mainly in S Illinois and W Kentucky. The few in SE Missouri approached far western counties. After several chaser videos of tornadoes west of Carter County, the cold front caught up to the storm and Carter County primarily had straight-line wind damage as a result, with a brief EF-0 tornado. Another tornado, an EF-1, entered Bollinger County southeast of Marquand. Several straight-line wind reports were received, along with more flash flooding as lingering high water and saturated soils from the day were quickly reinvigorated. 2-5 inches of rain fell across the southern two rows of counties, with some of the highest rainfall totals near the Black River and south of Cape Girardeau. More power outages occurred with Carter and Bollinger County outages, along with the seven counties from the morning with substantial outage numbers, continuing through the night. The Black River briefly flooded in Poplar Bluff following this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding briefly occurred at Poplar Bluff on the Black River with bottomlands affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter weather impacted the Missouri Bootheel on January 14-15, 2024. Transportation was impaired across the area. Snow spread across the Mid-South during the afternoon hours on January 14th as an upper jet intensified across the region in advance of a potent upper trough. The heaviest snow was located south of the Bootheel with accumulations of less than two inches in Dunklin and Pemiscot counties. Snow gradually tapered off on January 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than two inches of snow fell across Dunklin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy shallow dense fog formed over northern portions of Southeast Missouri during the early morning hours of the 18th. Surface winds were weak with high pressure moving in, while temperatures cooled to the crossover temperature. Surface moisture was plentiful with recent rainfall. Greater coverage/duration of fog occurred near rivers, such as at Cape Girardeau ASOS, while cloud cover prevented fog formation in the southernmost row of counties.

Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred on May 8, 2024. The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms affected the Missouri Ozarks beginning on the evening of June 8th and continued through the morning of June 9th. Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred with both rounds. Significant flash flooding occurred overnight where 3-6 inches of rainfall occurred. Numerous water rescues occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail fell in Hickory County on Highway D.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front pushed through the Missouri Ozarks during the late evening of May 6, 2024 and into the early morning hours of May 7, 2024. Thunderstorms developed along the front west of the Ozarks, quickly growing upscale to a linear storm mode by the time they reached southwest Missouri. This line of thunderstorms pushed through the area, causing widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of tree limbs and power poles broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of broken tree limbs that were 1 inch in diameter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles and trees were reported down west of Cassville and Butterfield in Barry County.  These winds continued to cause damage to trees extending east and southeast through Shell Knob, Roaring River and Eagle Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening on May 26, 2024. Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in early May, culminating in the May 8th severe weather and flooding event, resulted in rising river on the Mississippi River, with Thebes returning to minor flood and Hickman reaching minor flood in the following days. Minor flooding in Southeast Missouri around these river sites lasted roughly a week in both areas with impacts limited to low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of low-lying areas across from Hickman occurred mid-month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several rounds of severe storms on May 7th, 2024. An early morning line of storms brought wind damage to a large portion of Iowa, Illinois, and northeast Missouri. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills were 0 degrees to 15 below zero at times.

Thunderstorms developed along the front west of the Ozarks, quickly growing upscale to a linear storm mode by the time they reached southwest Missouri. This line of thunderstorms pushed through the area, causing widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four large trees toppled or split.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Missouri Ozarks during the early morning of May 4, 2024 bringing strong thunderstorms to the area. Most thunderstorms produced sub-severe hail between dime and nickel size, however quarter size hail was reported in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported 3 miles west of Foose in Polk County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred on May 8, 2024. The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of half dollar size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms affected the Missouri Ozarks beginning on the evening of June 8th and continued through the morning of June 9th. Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred with both rounds. Significant flash flooding occurred overnight where 3-6 inches of rainfall occurred. Numerous water rescues occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down blocking farm road 5 west of Walnut Grove.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed and knocked over powerlines west of Willard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening on May 26, 2024. Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down blocking road on County Rd 253.

For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system that originated in the Plains brought an associated cold front through the Missouri Ozarks during the late afternoon and evening hours on May 21, 2024. Discreet supercells quickly congealed into a linear mode, producing hail between quarter and ping pong ball size, and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of multiple trees down and damage to an outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route Y west and eastbound was closed due to flooding.
