EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening on May 26, 2024. Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of ping pong ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front pushed through the Missouri Ozarks during the late evening of May 6, 2024 and into the early morning hours of May 7, 2024. Thunderstorms developed along the front west of the Ozarks, quickly growing upscale to a linear storm mode by the time they reached southwest Missouri. This line of thunderstorms pushed through the area, causing widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of tree limbs broken and shingles blown off.

The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy shallow dense fog formed over northern portions of Southeast Missouri during the early morning hours of the 18th. Surface winds were weak with high pressure moving in, while temperatures cooled to the crossover temperature. Surface moisture was plentiful with recent rainfall. Greater coverage/duration of fog occurred near rivers, such as at Cape Girardeau ASOS, while cloud cover prevented fog formation in the southernmost row of counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening on May 26, 2024. Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of telephone poles and trees down on the east side of Ash Grove.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey concluded that an EF-1 tornado occurred in far southeast Oregon County to the east of Myrtle. Two homes suffered siding damage, five barns/outbuildings were destroyed, along with numerous trees suffering damage that ranged from limb damage to being uprooted along County Road 288 before the tornado crossed the Eleven Point River near the Myrtle Access and crossed into Ripley County north of County Road 286. One injury was reported. Damage cost estimates only include the Oregon County portion of the tornado track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey concluded that an EF-0 tornado started over the Caney Mountain Conservation Area east of Willhoit and travelled northeast for almost 5 miles. The tornado uprooted several trees and snapped large limbs along its path. A tornado debris signature was present on radar for the duration of this tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening on May 26, 2024. Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size hail 3 miles north of Edgar Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.25 inches was reported as the three hour total.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small branches were reported down at Highway D and 1st street. 60 mph wind gusts were estimated, with dime size hail observed.

Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system that originated in the Plains brought an associated cold front through the Missouri Ozarks during the late afternoon and evening hours on May 21, 2024. Discreet supercells quickly congealed into a linear mode, producing hail between quarter and ping pong ball size, and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a tree down on Colorado Avenue in Eldon that hit 2 vehicles and downed powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system that originated in the Plains brought an associated cold front through the Missouri Ozarks during the late afternoon and evening hours on May 21, 2024. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell and took down powerlines in Eldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3.19 inches was measured in a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred on May 8, 2024. The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of hail 3 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dynamic environment and low level jet helped ongoing convection in the Plains and western Missouri keep its strength as it propagated east into the forecast area. Through the early morning hours, two thunderstorms produced wind damage, one in Boone County, and the other in Reynolds and Iron Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked a large tree over the roadway along S Palis Nichols Road near E Webster Road near Ashland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front pushed through the Missouri Ozarks during the late evening of May 6, 2024 and into the early morning hours of May 7, 2024. Thunderstorms developed along the front west of the Ozarks, quickly growing upscale to a linear storm mode by the time they reached southwest Missouri. This line of thunderstorms pushed through the area, causing widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route YY closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired along a cold front on the afternoon of the 4th. A high instability and low shear environment yielded disorganized convection. Regardless, isolated severe thunderstorms were observed in Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed and there was tree damage in and around Highway 21 and County Road 27 just north of Belleview. Power poles were snapped on Highway 21 just northwest of Pilot Knob.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex was situated over the border of southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri during the early afternoon hours on May 31, 2024 before slowly continuing to push northeast over the Missouri Ozarks during the late afternoon/evening and into the overnight hours into June 1, 2024. As this MCV pushed through the Missouri Ozarks, a band of moderate to heavy rain developed along a locally-modified baroclinic boundary, producing a considerable amount of rain over portions of Douglas, Wright, Polk, and Shannon counties. This includes locations that received well over 6 inches of rainfall near Mansfield in Wright County. Additional flooding and heavy rain occurred during the early morning hours on June 1, 2024. Those entries are included in June's portion of Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3.92 inches was reported as the 24 hour measurement, with most of that falling in the last 6 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Survey team found EF-0 damage in northern Barry County as numerous large trees were uprooted. As the tornado continued to track northeast, it intensified to an EF-1 and caused damage to an outbuilding as well as the roofing material to a house just before crossing over the Barry/Lawrence County line, where it continued to track through southern Lawrence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South was pinched in the warm sector of two frontal boundaries in the early afternoon hours allowing a few strong to severe thunderstorms across Tennessee. A strong cold front aimed to cross the Mid-South later that evening with a primed environment that featured steep lapse rates, plentiful instability, curved hodographs, and plenty of effective shear. Strong upper level divergence and an intensifying low level jet combined with the environment to produce damaging winds and large hail. Convection continued until shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree is down on Patty Lynn Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The bi-state region was threatened with severe thunderstorms on May 8th. Numerous supercells produced very large hail most of the day, and a small QLCS produced multiple tornadoes in southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 1 to 1.75 inch hail occurred between Leadwood and Desloge and south to Farmington. Additional cities include Park Hills and Leadington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front pushed through the Missouri Ozarks during the late evening of May 6, 2024 and into the early morning hours of May 7, 2024. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined that an EF-0 tornado with maximum wind speeds of 80 mph touched down in Greene County and continued tracking northeast. The tornado both uprooted trees and downed large limbs out of numerous other trees, and damaged or destroyed several outbuildings along an intermittent path extending from Greene Hills Country Club northwest of Willard across northern Greene County before reaching the Greene/Polk County line. Two homes were damaged by falling trees, with one injury reported in one of these homes. The tornado continued to track into Polk and Dallas counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of an EF-0 tornado that touched down in Greene County. Once the tornado crossed into Polk County, it continued it's intermittent path northeast, intermittently uprooting trees and causing limb damage for 3.7 miles before crossing into Dallas County.

Once it crossed the Phelps/Maries County line, it continues its intermittent path for 1.07 miles, uprooting a few trees before lifting 5 miles northeast of Dixon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening of May 26, 2024, ten tornadoes touched down in the St. Louis metropolitan area and southwest Illinois from a mix of supercells and QLCS modes. This occurred alongside numerous reports of large hail of up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging winds of up to 81 mph. Thankfully, there were no reported injuries or fatalities with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell caused a consistent swath of wind damage across southern St. Louis County. Damage included large limbs and trees downed, topped, and snapped, trees down on homes, a central air conditioning unit blown over, a metal backyard canopy lofted into another yard, and significant damage to shingles. Communities impacted include Eureka, Glencoe, Crescent, Sherman, Jedburgh, Valley Park, Peerless Park, Fenton, Concord, Mattese, Mehlville, Sappington, Affton, Lemay, and Oakville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening of May 26, 2024, ten tornadoes touched down in the St. Louis metropolitan area and southwest Illinois from a mix of supercells and QLCS modes. This occurred alongside numerous reports of large hail of up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging winds of up to 81 mph. Thankfully, there were no reported injuries or fatalities with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds reportedly downed and snapped trees and large branches in and around Gray Summit and Pacific.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy shallow dense fog formed over northern portions of Southeast Missouri during the early morning hours of the 18th. Surface winds were weak with high pressure moving in, while temperatures cooled to the crossover temperature. Surface moisture was plentiful with recent rainfall. Greater coverage/duration of fog occurred near rivers, such as at Cape Girardeau ASOS, while cloud cover prevented fog formation in the southernmost row of counties.

Heavy rainfall in early May, culminating in the May 8th severe weather and flooding event, resulted in rising river on the Mississippi River, with Thebes returning to minor flood and Hickman reaching minor flood in the following days. Minor flooding in Southeast Missouri around these river sites lasted roughly a week in both areas with impacts limited to low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Mississippi River at Thebes in low-lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete thunderstorms developed in the late morning of May 24th in the free warm sector ahead of an approaching cold front. One thunderstorm produced large hail and damaging wind in central Missouri. Later in the evening, a supercell produced large hail in Jefferson County.

A weak warm front produced convection as it moved north across bi-state area. Thunderstorms were expected to be disorganized and largely sub-severe. This was mostly true in the afternoon and early evening aside from one report of wind damage in St. Francois County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10 to 12 inch diameter tree was downed on King School Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Loosely organized multi-cell thunderstorms moved into central Missouri and intensified during the afternoon of the 2nd. One thunderstorm became severe and produced up to golf ball sized hail in Marion County before crossing into Illinois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening hours of April 27, severe storms impacted much of the WFO Kansas City county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF1 tornado began near or just west of U.S. Highway 169 south of Gentry, where a tractor trailer was blown over. Little damage was observed as the tornado moved northeast to 558 Road. A residence at this location featured roof damage to the home, damage to multiple grain bins, and complete destruction of a small barn. Multiple tree limbs were downed in the area, and debris from inside the barn was strewn around the property. The tornado continued northeast from this location, but little damaged was observed beyond this point. The end point is estimated based on spotter reports of the tornado crossing into Harrison County before lifting, though it is possible the tornado ended before this.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South was pinched in the warm sector of two frontal boundaries in the early afternoon hours allowing a few strong to severe thunderstorms across Tennessee. A strong cold front aimed to cross the Mid-South later that evening with a primed environment that featured steep lapse rates, plentiful instability, curved hodographs, and plenty of effective shear. Strong upper level divergence and an intensifying low level jet combined with the environment to produce damaging winds and large hail. Convection continued until shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball sized hail (2.75 inches) fell east of the Cash Memorial Conservation Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred on May 8, 2024. The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Aurora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved through the region on May 13, 2024. Thunderstorms developed in the warm sector ahead of the low pressure during the afternoon and evening, producing damaging winds in Ozark County and heavy rain in Greene County. Additionally, a brief EF-0 tornado touched down at the Payne's Valley Golf Course southeast of Hollister, snapping a few small trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado touched down at the Payne Valley Golf Course to the southeast of Hollister in Taney County. A NWS storm survey found broken tree branches and a few small trees snapped. No damage to homes or powerlines. No direct injuries due to the tornado. Maximum winds estimated at 70 mph, with a path length of 1.23 miles and a width of 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening on May 26, 2024. Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of two inch hail in Marionville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front pushed through the Missouri Ozarks during the late evening of May 6, 2024 and into the early morning hours of May 7, 2024. Thunderstorms developed along the front west of the Ozarks, quickly growing upscale to a linear storm mode by the time they reached southwest Missouri. This line of thunderstorms pushed through the area, causing widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey determined a short lived EF-0 tornado touched down in this location. Maximum winds of 70-75 mph caused trees to be down in multiple directions. The storm continued to produce non-tornadic wind damage as it moved east of Highway 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along a cold front, with rotation developing in Jasper County near Joplin. Areas of rotation continued to track through Jasper and Barton counties, with a confirmed tornado in Jasper County near Carl Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey confirmed an EF-1 tornado with maximum wind speeds of 90 mph uprooted and or damaged numerous trees, including one that caused significant damage to a home as it fell. Four additional homes had roof and or siding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex was situated over the border of southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri during the early afternoon hours on May 31, 2024 before slowly continuing to push northeast over the Missouri Ozarks during the late afternoon/evening and into the overnight hours into June 1, 2024. As this MCV pushed through the Missouri Ozarks, a band of moderate to heavy rain developed along a locally-modified baroclinic boundary, producing a considerable amount of rain over portions of Douglas, Wright, Polk, and Shannon counties. This includes locations that received well over 6 inches of rainfall near Mansfield in Wright County. Additional flooding and heavy rain occurred during the early morning hours on June 1, 2024. Those entries are included in June's portion of Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route NN was closed due to flooding of Rocky Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening of May 26, 2024, ten tornadoes touched down in the St. Louis metropolitan area and southwest Illinois from a mix of supercells and QLCS modes. This occurred alongside numerous reports of large hail of up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging winds of up to 81 mph. Thankfully, there were no reported injuries or fatalities with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down at the intersection of Highway K and County Road 310, almost a mile south of Redford, snapping off several large tree limbs. Then the tornado tracked to the northeast. More large tree limbs were snapped off as it crossed Highway O just east of Redford. As it approached Highway K again, it snapped off more large tree limbs before lifting and dissipating around 2.8 miles east northeast of Redford. Overall, the tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 3.27 miles and a max path width of 276 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge and approaching shortwave trough surged moisture across the area to allow dewpoints to creep into the low to mid 70s with hot conditions in place. Fortunately, convection and excellent mixing allowed heat indices to remain below excessive levels. Nonetheless, hot and humid conditions persisted ahead of a cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices between 105 and 109 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening hours of July 31st, an MCS brought severe wind gusts to the KC metro. Wind gusts up to 80 mph were reported on the Kansas side of the KC metro, but winds had weakened to 60 to 70 mph through the Missouri side of the KC metro. Widespread power outages were reported. A large percentage of the 60+ mph winds were associated with the gust front/outflow boundary out ahead of the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late evening hours of May 6th and early morning hours of May 7th, several severe linear features moved through Missouri from west to east. These produced widespread 60 mph straight line wind damage with numerous embedded QLCS mesovortices and a few confirmed QLCS tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey confirmed a 5.5 mile long EF-1 tornado associated with a QLCS that began near the intersection of Missouri 7 Highway and Wyatt Road in south Blue Springs where it destroyed a small gazebo in a residential area. The tornado then continued to the northeast, where it damaged several commercial buildings on SW Eagles Parkway in Grain Valley. The tornado then crossed Interstate 70 in Grain Valley, where it blew over a semi truck and flipped two RV trailers from an RV dealership into a ditch. The damage path ended just north of Interstate 70 in Grain Valley. Estimated wind speeds were as high as 100 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began just east of Route YY and just south of State Highway 52 southeast of Butler in Bates County, where minor tree damage was observed. The tornado strengthened as it moved east-northeast toward SH 52, snapping or uprooting several trees at a residence just south of the highway. The fallen trees were in a variety of directions, confirming the presence of a tornado. The tornado continued along the highway east past Route N, where power poles were damaged and additional minor tree damage was observed. The last signs of damage were just east of Route N, where small limbs were downed at a residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level high-pressure system covered the Lower Mississippi Valley at the end of July. Persistent moist southwest flow brought high dewpoints into the region. Hot temperatures combined with dewpoints well into the 70s resulted in widespread afternoon heat index values above 110 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values topped 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning and early afternoon hours of July 1st, heavy rainfall yielded flash flooding across much of the southern two thirds of Jackson County. A swath of 3 to 6 of rain with isolated locations up to 7 fell within about a 3 hour time period from about 930 am to 1230 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of Jackson County within a span of a couple hours, causing widespread flash flooding across much of Jackson County. Major roadways were flooded and closed down, with several water rescues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow-moving storms occurred on the afternoon of June 25, lasting into the evening. These storms quickly became strong to severe, with multiple becoming supercells, which ended up producing locally heavy rainfall, damaging winds, large hail, and four tornadoes that were generally weak, but still capable of damage. Winds were generally 60 mph or greater with the stronger storms, with a corridor of winds greater than 65 mph. These thunderstorms remained nearly stationary in areas, resulting in nearly 2 to 4 inches of quick rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 6 inch diameter tree branch downed by the thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind, large hail, and five tornadoes to the Missouri Ozarks from the evening of April 1st to the early morning hours of April 2nd. A tornado that occurred near Fair Grove was rated in EF-1. The four other tornadoes were rated EF-0 with three of them occurring from eastern Dallas County into Laclede County. The fourth EF-0 tornado occurred in St. James.||In addition to the tornadoes, storm surveys revealed several different swaths of damaging straight line wind speeds of 70-100 mph. Wind speeds of 90-100 mph caused roof damage to businesses in downtown Carthage with power poles and trees also snapped. Other swaths of significant wind damage occurred near Halltown, Elkland, and along the I-44 corridor from Waynesville to Rolla.||Several thunderstorms also produced hail from the size of quarters to tennis balls. Hail ranging from one to two inches in diameter occurred in an around Bolivar. Tennis ball size hail fell in the town of Maple Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey determined that straight line winds up to 75 mph uprooted a large tree which fell on a home. Another residence had minor roof damage and multiple cedar trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind, large hail, and five tornadoes to the Missouri Ozarks from the evening of April 1st to the early morning hours of April 2nd. A tornado that occurred near Fair Grove was rated in EF-1. The four other tornadoes were rated EF-0 with three of them occurring from eastern Dallas County into Laclede County. The fourth EF-0 tornado occurred in St. James.||In addition to the tornadoes, storm surveys revealed several different swaths of damaging straight line wind speeds of 70-100 mph. Wind speeds of 90-100 mph caused roof damage to businesses in downtown Carthage with power poles and trees also snapped. Other swaths of significant wind damage occurred near Halltown, Elkland, and along the I-44 corridor from Waynesville to Rolla.||Several thunderstorms also produced hail from the size of quarters to tennis balls. Hail ranging from one to two inches in diameter occurred in an around Bolivar. Tennis ball size hail fell in the town of Maple Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey indicated an EF-0 tornado with peak winds of 80 mph uprooted or snapped over 100 trees as it moved east southeast across extreme eastern Dallas before crossing into Laclede County. Along its path in Dallas County the tornado rolled an unanchored mobile home, damaged the roof of a residence, and damaged multiple outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind, large hail, and five tornadoes to the Missouri Ozarks from the evening of April 1st to the early morning hours of April 2nd. A tornado that occurred near Fair Grove was rated in EF-1. The four other tornadoes were rated EF-0 with three of them occurring from eastern Dallas County into Laclede County. The fourth EF-0 tornado occurred in St. James.||In addition to the tornadoes, storm surveys revealed several different swaths of damaging straight line wind speeds of 70-100 mph. Wind speeds of 90-100 mph caused roof damage to businesses in downtown Carthage with power poles and trees also snapped. Other swaths of significant wind damage occurred near Halltown, Elkland, and along the I-44 corridor from Waynesville to Rolla.||Several thunderstorms also produced hail from the size of quarters to tennis balls. Hail ranging from one to two inches in diameter occurred in an around Bolivar. Tennis ball size hail fell in the town of Maple Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees with diameters of 6 to 16 inches were uprooted, snapped or had tops twisted out of them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable air mass across the region ahead of a southward sinking cold front resulted in thunderstorm development, with several being severe. These produced large hail and scattered damaging winds. Most hail reports ranged in size from quarter to golf ball size hail. The largest hail fell in Van Buren County near Keosauqua where 2 to 3 inch diameter hail fell. There were also a few reports of 60 to 80 MPH across far southeast Iowa into west central Illinois, which damaged trees and knocked down several outbuildings.  The most widespread damage occurred near Oakville, IA.  Two brief, non-damaging tornadoes were also seen north of Macomb, IL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 6 AM on May 8th, storms initiated in far eastern Kansas just west of the Missouri state line. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow-moving storms occurred on the afternoon of June 25, lasting into the evening. These storms quickly became strong to severe, with multiple becoming supercells, which ended up producing locally heavy rainfall, damaging winds, large hail, and four tornadoes that were generally weak, but still capable of damage. Winds were generally 60 mph or greater with the stronger storms, with a corridor of winds greater than 65 mph. These thunderstorms remained nearly stationary in areas, resulting in nearly 2 to 4 inches of quick rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree blown down over Highway 15. In addition, a portable shed was blown from parking lot of store, onto the highway, where it was hit by a truck and destroyed. Photos sent confirming damage, and time estimated from radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from baseball to softball in size fell to the northwest, north and east of Bolivar. 200 homes at an average of $32,000 suffered damages to siding, roofs and windows, and 350 cars were damaged, many totaled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of Sunday, May 26th, storms developed over northern Missouri on the backside of a surface low, moving northwest to southeast through the area, producing large hail and a few damaging wind gusts. The town of Macon was hit twice by two separate storms, with the first bringing reported measured hailstones of 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 60 mph in the community of Ravanna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of June 25th, semi-discrete convection ahead of a cold front produced widespread wind damage in central and northeast Missouri. Unfortunately, this resulted in one fatality in Audrain County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One direct fatality occurred 5 miles north of Martinsburg when an old, dilapidated barn on Highway 54 collapsed on an individual seeking shelter from the thunderstorm. Large trees and tree limbs were blown down in Vandalia, Laddonia, and Mexico. Siding was also blown off a church south of Mexico Airport (MYJ), power poles were knocked down by tree limbs in Vandalia, and a roof was blown off a trailer in Laddonia. Other communities in the storm's path are Scott's Corner, Rush Hill, and Martinsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon of June 13th ahead of an approaching cold front. Supercells initially produced large hail in northeast Missouri before transitioning into mostly a wind threat as they neared the Mississippi River. Further south, semi-discrete convection produced damaging winds in Boone County later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage of varying degrees throughout Lewis County. The most extensive damage occurred in and around Ewing, La Grange, and Canton where trees and power lines were downed. Additional cities impacted were La Belle, and Maywood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported roof damage to a mobile home and large tree limbs down near Tebbetts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind, large hail, and five tornadoes to the Missouri Ozarks from the evening of April 1st to the early morning hours of April 2nd. A tornado that occurred near Fair Grove was rated in EF-1. The four other tornadoes were rated EF-0 with three of them occurring from eastern Dallas County into Laclede County. The fourth EF-0 tornado occurred in St. James.||In addition to the tornadoes, storm surveys revealed several different swaths of damaging straight line wind speeds of 70-100 mph. Wind speeds of 90-100 mph caused roof damage to businesses in downtown Carthage with power poles and trees also snapped. Other swaths of significant wind damage occurred near Halltown, Elkland, and along the I-44 corridor from Waynesville to Rolla.||Several thunderstorms also produced hail from the size of quarters to tennis balls. Hail ranging from one to two inches in diameter occurred in an around Bolivar. Tennis ball size hail fell in the town of Maple Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey indicated an EF-0 tornado with peak winds of 80 mph uprooted or|snapped over 100 trees as it moved east southeast out of extreme eastern Dallas County and continued across extreme western Laclede Counties before lifting near the town of north northwest of Phillipsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell developed in the late evening of June 8th as upper level ascent passed over southern Missouri. This supercell caused wind damage in Reynolds County before exiting the forecast area to the southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous large trees along County Road 806 from Sutton Bluff Recreation Area to Centerville. Also, several power lines were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms affected the Missouri Ozarks beginning on the evening of June 8th and continued through the morning of June 9th. Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred with both rounds. Significant flash flooding occurred overnight where 3-6 inches of rainfall occurred. Numerous water rescues occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway BB was closed due to flooding of the Big Piney River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortly after midnight, supercells developed over northern Missouri, producing large hail. Then later in the morning, a squall line moved eastward out of WFO Omaha's area and into NW Missouri, producing some measured wind gusts up to 65 mph and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station measured 63 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 21st, strong to severe storms developed over far eastern Kansas, and moved into western Missouri. These storms produced some hail, damaging wind gusts, and one tornado in Henry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 5:26 PM CDT a brief EF-0 Tornado occurred east of Calhoun, MO just north of MO Hwy OO. The tornado lifted the roof off of a metal outbuilding, tossing the metal sides and roof to the north and northwest as well as some of the lighter contents of the structure. The tornado continued to track northeast for 0.35 miles, resulting in minor tree debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of Sunday, May 26th, storms developed over northern Missouri on the backside of a surface low, moving northwest to southeast through the area, producing large hail and a few damaging wind gusts. The town of Macon was hit twice by two separate storms, with the first bringing reported measured hailstones of 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Mercer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon hours of May 26th, supercells produced large hail to portions of Bates and Henry Counties in the southern reaches of the WFO Pleasant Hill CWA. Baseball sized hail was reported east of Clinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail up to golf ball size was reported in Clinton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1420 CST, emergency management in Worth County Missouri reported 1.25 inch hail in the town of Sheridan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed within a very unstable environment on the afternoon of June 3rd. Very little upper level ascent and deep layer shear limited the potential for more than isolated strong to severe thunderstorms. One thunderstorm did become severe, causing wind damage in Boone County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree fell on a mobile home and on a vehicle near Columbia. Another large tree was blown down onto Owens School Road near its intersection with Highway Z.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arrival of a cold front and upper level ascent allowed ongoing pre-frontal convection to strengthen in the pre-dawn hours. One thunderstorm became severe, causing wind damage in Pike County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported one tree down near County Road 272 and Highway 54.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late evening hours of May 6th and early morning hours of May 7th, several severe linear features moved through Missouri from west to east. These produced widespread 60 mph straight line wind damage with numerous embedded QLCS mesovortices and a few confirmed QLCS tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of straight-line wind damage occurred near a mesovortex that developed within a line of thunderstorms on the evening of May 6, beginning near Piper in Wyandotte County. Several trees were downed along a path from Donahoo Road to Polfer Road, crossing 123rd Street between Polfer Road and Marxen Road. In this area, several larger trees and limbs were downed, with a couple uprooted. The swath of winds progressed toward Wolcott Drive and Tontine Road, where several trees were downed, snapped, or uprooted. A private weather station reported a 60-mph gust in this area at this time, but stronger winds were likely present given the nature of the tree damage. The damaging winds crossed the Missouri River and reached Missouri State Highway 45 north of Waldron, where multiple small trees or limbs were damaged as well. No discernible damage was noted to the northeast of this area. Damage along the path was all directed to the north- northeast, which corroborates with radar data from the Terminal Doppler Radar at TMCI. This straight-line wind event preceded the tornado that eventually developed near Hoover in Platte County a few minutes later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey concluded that an EF-1 tornado with peak winds of 95 mph occurred near Fair Grove, Missouri. The width of the tornado was 100 yards. The tornado remained on the ground for around two miles while uprooting and snapping several trees. The tornado heavily damaged an outbuilding while also causing minor damage to a residence. A tornado debris signature was also present on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports came in from the public for hailstones of 1 to 2.75 inches in diameter. The largest stones fell near La Belle. Additional cities impacted include Lewistown, Monticello, and Ewing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred on May 8, 2024. The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of hen egg size hail.

Training heavy rainfall through the morning hours in Southeast Missouri resulted in 3 to 5 inches of rainfall in much of Butler County and portions of Wayne, Bollinger, and Stoddard Counties. Considerable flash flooding issues developed in Poplar Bluff, which had vehicles stranded in several areas and up to half a foot of water in a high school hallway. MLCAPE was around 500 J/kg, precipitable water was 1.9-2.2 inches, and 850 mb moisture transport peaked around 0800CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential early morning rainfall resulted in street flooding in Poplar Bluff, while subsequent rounds of heavy rainfall intensified flooding issues. Multiple motorists were stranded in several locations across the city. Multiple city roads were closed including Westwood Ave/Bus U.S. 67. Poplar Bluff High School had close to 6 inches of water in one of the hallways. Poplar Bluff Municipal Airport received 3.89 inches of rainfall between 0500-0900CST while a spotter reported 5.08 inches of rain near Westwood Hills Country Club through 1000CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary tracking southward from previous storms produced convection during the evening in Southeast Missouri, with additional storms developing ahead of the boundary. Several thunderstorm wind damage reports were received. Notably, an isolated cluster of damage involved a line of 8 power poles toppled over between Charleston and Bertrand due to an isolated thunderstorm outflow boundary. A more extended path of damage occurred as a storm cluster tracked from Jackson to Cape Girardeau, damaging numerous trees and powerlines. MLCAPE was 2500-3500 J/kg during the early evening while effective bulk shear peaked at around 30 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree down and numerous large branches were downed by thunderstorm winds at the Castor River Campground on Hwy Y.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system originated over the Dakotas before pivoting and becoming negatively tilted during the early morning hours on May 24, 2024. An associated surface cold front pushed through the Missouri Ozarks during the afternoon, bringing scattered thunderstorms producing hail between quarter and hen egg size as well as isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of hail ranging between quarter and tennis ball size was reported in Dixon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of hail 2.25 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level ridging prevailed from the southwest U.S. eastward across the southern states. Moisture pooled along a stalled frontal boundary across the Mid-South with dewpoints rising into the upper 70s. The abundance of moisture combined with temperatures around 100 degrees resulted in widespread high heat indices.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in between 105 and 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the heavy rainfall event that started May 31. A mesoscale convective vortex continued to produce heavy rainfall across portions of southern Missouri where localized amounts over 6 inches occurred. This caused flooding of low water crossings and caused rivers to rise into flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water is over the road and impassable at Creek Road and Jones Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south from northern Missouri during the evening hours of June 8, 2024. A mesoscale convective system developed along and in advance of the front in the moist and unstable airmass across southern Missouri. There was enough shear across the area to support a few severe storms. This convection pushed across northern parts of the Mid-South mainly during the early morning hours of June 9, 2024, with isolated reports of hail and damaging winds.

Mostly clear skies and plenty of moisture surging in from the south allowed for heat indices to reach 105 to 113 degrees across Southeast Missouri. The highest observed heat index in Southeast Missouri was 113 degrees at the Cape Girardeau Regional Airport ASOS site. An outflow boundary advancing southward during the late afternoon into the early evening brought an end to heat concerns for the day.

Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred on May 8, 2024. The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of hail sizes ranging between golf balls and tennis balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge prevailed across the region in late June. A cold front had stalled just to the north of the area and moisture continued to surge into the Mid-South allowing dewpoints to rise into the upper 70s and lower 80s resulting in triple-digit heat indices.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in excess of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening on May 26, 2024. Supercell thunderstorms initialized over central Missouri and spread southward as rapid destabilization occurred along and east of Highway 65. 40-55 knots of deep layer shear combined with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient instability primed the atmosphere to produce all modes of severe weather. This included hail up to 4 inches in diameter, which caused extensive damage to cars and roofs in Webster County, as well as damaging winds and flooding. Four tornadoes touched down, including an EF-2 tornado in Shannon County, and an EF-3 tornado in Howell and Shannon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of tennis ball size hail northwest of Warsaw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon convection developed and moved northeastward through the Quad State from the mid afternoon to just after sunset as a shortwave disturbance progressed across Arkansas. The town of Portageville in New Madrid County experienced wind gusts to 55 mph and penny size hail. MLCAPE was 2000-2500 J/kg and precipitable water was 1.7-1.8 inches, but effective bulk shear was limited to around 20 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail occurred in Portageville. A social media video from the town showed |wind gusts estimated to 50 to 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow zone of training rainfall early morning on the 5th resulted in localized flooding issues in northwestern Stoddard County, Missouri. Convective activity decreased later in the morning. MLCAPE was 1000 J/kg while effective bulk shear was 25-30 kts and precipitable water was 1.7-1.9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred at the intersection of WW and County Rd 420. Radar estimated rainfall amounts during the early morning hours were 1.25-2 inches in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection developed during the afternoon hours in the Quad State, with storms tracking northeastward. Damage occurred to a barn and some trees in Scott County, Missouri. MLCAPE was around 3000 J/kg while effective bulk shear was 25-30 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downdraft winds damaged the roof of a house and trees along MO-449 near Old Bethel Baptist Church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture surged into the region while a strong upper ridge prevailed over the ArkLaTex region. Surface dewpoints climbed into the low to middle 70s while high temperatures rose into the lower to middle 90s resulting in high heat indices. The aforementioned upper ridge continued to intensify resulting in a couple of days of dangerous heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in excess of 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through the day of April 1, a warm front left much of the area in a warm, moist, and increasingly unstable airmass. This environment supported multiple waves of severe thunderstorms from the evening of April 1st through the early morning of April 2nd. Notable reports include large hail up to 2 inches in diameter, isolated damaging winds, a brief EF-0 tornado, and instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of tree damage in Chesterfield from downed branches to snapped and uprooted trees. Damage to power lines and poles, missing shingles, and a damaged sign also reported.

Extensive, widespread damage to cars was reported with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of April 18, 2024, discrete supercell thunderstorms developed along a cold front moving through the bi-state area. These storms were initially capable of producing large hail and damaging wind gusts, but became tornadic as additional thunderstorms formed along the front. Due to the additional thunderstorms, storms congealed into a QLCS that impacted the St. Louis metro area. The QLCS was responsible for a tornado and straight line wind damage in this area. Fortunately, no injuries or fatalities were reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of tree limbs and power poles broken with power lines down. There was one report of a traffic line blown down. Some reported shingles blown off of roofs, while one reported their entire roof had blown off. There were also reports of multiple large trees snapped and uprooted around the area with one falling on a home. Communities within the path of this storm were Valley Park, Fenton, Sunset Hills, Sappington, Concord, Mehlville, and Oakville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of rain and a cold frontal passage, Southeast Missouri had clearing skies and calming winds for the overnight hours early on the 30th. Dense fog developed shortly after midnight, remaining until mid-morning.

Early morning, the surface low was located just north of St. Louis, Missouri, lifting to the NNE as it rapidly deepened. For Southeast Missouri, early morning effective bulk shear was around 60 kts, with a very high 25-30 kts of 0-1 km shear, while SRHs were around 200 m2/s2. MLCAPE was 1000-1500 J/kg, and mid-level lapse rates were 6.5 C/km. A 70-90 kt mid-level jet was west of the Quad State. A strong capping inversion suppressed storm activity in the evening, but the strong lift and upper level diffluence won out early morning. Two lines of storms developed after midnight in Southeast Missouri. The first had its southern end move through Perry County from 0100-0230CST. The latter was a very potent thunderstorm line racing eastward early morning April 2nd. Weaker thunderstorm activity occurred further south in the Ozark Foothills and near the bootheel. This line continued throughout the day all the way to West Virginia, being designated a derecho. For Southeast Missouri, 1 brief EF-0 tornado occurred west of Cape Girardeau, which transitioned into a microburst that progressed through residential areas in western Cape Girardeau city. Other instances of straight-line wind damage occurred in Cape Girardeau, Scott, and Stoddard Counties. According to county EMA, Cape Girardeau County had a total of 2 barns and 12 houses with reported damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado uprooted a few trees and downed several large tree limbs along County Rd 319 in central Cape Girardeau County during the early morning hours. The tornadic circulation quickly diminished as the storm moved east, but continued to produce sporadic straight line wind damage as it moved east-southeast into the west side of Cape Girardeau. Peak winds were estimated at 80 mph.

This caused flooding of low water crossings and caused rivers to rise into flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State highway E, just north of Norwood was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of light winds and clearing skies behind a cold frontal passage produced fog in portions of Southeast Missouri during the overnight hours, dissipating around sunrise as winds increased.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with peak winds of 85 mph touched down just northeast of the Interstate 64 and Highway 340 interchange. The tornado traveled 2 miles east-northeast for approximately 3 minutes before lifting just west of Highway 141. Several tree limbs were damaged with minor damage to the roofs of multiple residences. Overall, the path length was 2.01 miles and the max path width was 332 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon, multicellular thunderstorms produced large hail spanning from Knox County to St. Charles County as they tracked northeast. Isolated bouts of wind damage were reported through the afternoon as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported numerous tree limbs down in Canton with the largest up to 6 inches in diameter. A 3 to 4 foot diameter tree was also downed near 4th Street and Lewis Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind, large hail, and five tornadoes to the Missouri Ozarks from the evening of April 1st to the early morning hours of April 2nd. A tornado that occurred near Fair Grove was rated in EF-1. The four other tornadoes were rated EF-0 with three of them occurring from eastern Dallas County into Laclede County. The fourth EF-0 tornado occurred in St. James.||In addition to the tornadoes, storm surveys revealed several different swaths of damaging straight line wind speeds of 70-100 mph. Wind speeds of 90-100 mph caused roof damage to businesses in downtown Carthage with power poles and trees also snapped. Other swaths of significant wind damage occurred near Halltown, Elkland, and along the I-44 corridor from Waynesville to Rolla.||Several thunderstorms also produced hail from the size of quarters to tennis balls. Hail ranging from one to two inches in diameter occurred in an around Bolivar. Tennis ball size hail fell in the town of Maple Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of golf balls was reported in the northern portions of Bolivar. Limited reports of damage to roofs and vehicles occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening upper low slowly drifting in the Southern Great Lakes to Northern Ohio Valley produced gusty winds out of the WNW across the Quad State. Wind gusts were 30-40 mph areawide for the midday to afternoon hours, with gusts to 43 mph occurring at both the Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff ASOS sites. Wrap-around showers occurred with small graupel occurring in many of these showers.

Early morning, the surface low was located just north of St. Louis, Missouri, lifting to the NNE as it rapidly deepened. For Southeast Missouri, early morning effective bulk shear was around 60 kts, with a very high 25-30 kts of 0-1 km shear, while SRHs were around 200 m2/s2. MLCAPE was 1000-1500 J/kg, and mid-level lapse rates were 6.5 C/km. A 70-90 kt mid-level jet was west of the Quad State. A strong capping inversion suppressed storm activity in the evening, but the strong lift and upper level diffluence won out early morning. Two lines of storms developed after midnight in Southeast Missouri. The first had its southern end move through Perry County from 0100-0230CST. The latter was a very potent thunderstorm line racing eastward early morning April 2nd. Weaker thunderstorm activity occurred further south in the Ozark Foothills and near the bootheel. This line continued throughout the day all the way to West Virginia, being designated a derecho. For Southeast Missouri, 1 brief EF-0 tornado occurred west of Cape Girardeau, which transitioned into a microburst that progressed through residential areas in western Cape Girardeau city. Other instances of straight-line wind damage occurred in Cape Girardeau, Scott, and Stoddard Counties. According to county EMA, Cape Girardeau County had a total of 2 barns and 12 houses with reported damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms downed multiple trees and power lines in Benton. As the storms continued eastward, trees were uprooted and power lines downed on MO-333 near the quarry and Sand Prairie Conservation Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex developed over Kansas during the early morning of August 13, 2024 and continued east towards southwest Missouri during the remainder of the morning. Despite the progressive nature of the system, heavy rainfall between 2 and 4.40 inches fell over Greene, Vernon, and Dade counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet station located four miles southeast of Glidewell in Greene County measured 3.52 inches of storm total rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward through the Quad State region during the morning hours. Dew points in the lower 70s made their way into Southeast Missouri, allowing MUCAPE to surge to 2000-2500 J/kg. SRH peaked around 150 m2/s2 in the evening with effective bulk shear around 40 kts. Lapse rates were 6-7 C/km. Cells formed in the afternoon, moving northeastward. The frontal passage moved through late evening. Wind damage reports in SEMO were concentrated on the path of a more north-south oriented portion of the line that showed rotation aloft. A funnel cloud observation was made northwest of Advance with that feature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds in Vanduser.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the early morning convection, low pressure remained northwest of the Quad State. Breezy southerly winds during the morning gave way to an early afternoon cold frontal passage which produced a few storms near Cape Girardeau. Gradient winds behind the front gusted to 40 mph at Cape Girardeau ASOS. Around noon, ahead of the front, far southeast Missouri had MLCAPE around 1000 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 70 kts, 0-3 km SRH of 200 m2/s2, and dew points in the upper 60s. After the early afternoon frontal passage, the environment quickly became unfavorable for storm development.

Eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri sat in the warm sector of an intense Spring storm system that brought widespread severe thunderstorms to the area during the afternoon and evening hours on April 16th. There were reports of hail, damaging winds, and two tornadoes. One of the tornadoes was on the ground for 42 miles across parts of Lee, Henry, Des Moines and Louisa Counties and was rated EF-2 with maximum winds of 130 MPH.

Early morning, the surface low was located just north of St. Louis, Missouri, lifting to the NNE as it rapidly deepened. For Southeast Missouri, early morning effective bulk shear was around 60 kts, with a very high 25-30 kts of 0-1 km shear, while SRHs were around 200 m2/s2. MLCAPE was 1000-1500 J/kg, and mid-level lapse rates were 6.5 C/km. A 70-90 kt mid-level jet was west of the Quad State. A strong capping inversion suppressed storm activity in the evening, but the strong lift and upper level diffluence won out early morning. Two lines of storms developed after midnight in Southeast Missouri. The first had its southern end move through Perry County from 0100-0230CST. The latter was a very potent thunderstorm line racing eastward early morning April 2nd. Weaker thunderstorm activity occurred further south in the Ozark Foothills and near the bootheel. This line continued throughout the day all the way to West Virginia, being designated a derecho. For Southeast Missouri, 1 brief EF-0 tornado occurred west of Cape Girardeau, which transitioned into a microburst that progressed through residential areas in western Cape Girardeau city. Other instances of straight-line wind damage occurred in Cape Girardeau, Scott, and Stoddard Counties. According to county EMA, Cape Girardeau County had a total of 2 barns and 12 houses with reported damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst with estimated peak winds of 85 mph caused sporadic wind damage on the west side of Cape Girardeau, MO, during the early morning hours of April 2, 2024. Dozens of trees were uprooted or suffered damage to large tree limbs. A few homes also had shingle, siding, or facia damage. One home on W Highland Dr had more than half of the roof removed while a home on Woodbine Pl suffered roof damage after a tree fell on it. The microburst started just east of the path of an EF-0 tornado along County Road 318. Crossing over Interstate 55, damage continued into the west side of Cape Girardeau. The microburst was about 3.25 miles long and about 400 yards in width for the main damage swath, with isolated tree damage to the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred on May 8, 2024. The first round occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours as a surface low pressure system approached the area from the southwest, pushing a strong frontal boundary and shortwave energy/lift into the region. For this round, thunderstorms were more elevated in nature, producing hail up to golf ball size, damaging winds, and flash flooding due to heavy rain.||The second round of severe thunderstorms occurred during the mid-afternoon and evening hours as stronger lift approached the frontal boundary and additional thunderstorms developed along the front. Supercells produced hail up to 4 inches in diameter, widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF-1 tornado in Barry and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of tennis ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level shortwave energy pushed through the region during the early morning hours of August 14, 2024, sparking a band of convection over portions of southwest Missouri. As a result, heavy rain developed, with around 5 inches of rainfall causing areal and flash flooding in Wright, Howell, Douglas, and Ozark counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway EE in northeast Douglas County was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of small creeks and streams and major flooding along the Marmaton and Little Osage River basins occurred thanks to heavy rainfall. From 2 to 4 inches of rainfall on already saturated ground from previous rainfall in the 25th. Isolated severe storms produced brief tornadoes in Vernon and St. Clair Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hayes Road at Greasy Creek was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms entered central and northeast Missouri overnight on April 27th, causing isolated bouts of wind damage and a single tornado before de-intensifying within a few hours of arrival.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along Alexander Road just east of intersection with Hays Road at 10:59 pm (northeast of Tipton, MO) snapping off several power poles. It tracked to the east snapping off several large trees, destroying an unoccupied mobile home, and causing minor roof damage to an outbuilding. Then the tornado veered, tracking to the northeast and causing damage to an open sided outbuilding after crossing over Cross Lane Road. The tornado soon lifted and dissipated just south of Old Twelve Highway. Overall, the tornado was rated EF1 with a path length of 1.70 miles and a max path width of 30 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

Eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri sat in the warm sector of an intense Spring storm system that brought widespread severe thunderstorms to the area during the afternoon and evening hours on April 16th. There were reports of hail, damaging winds, and two tornadoes. One of the tornadoes was on the ground for 42 miles across parts of Lee, Henry, Des Moines and Louisa Counties and was rated EF-2 with maximum winds of 130 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager relayed a 1 inch hail report by the Wyaconda Fire Department.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported dime to quarter size hail falling right now.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The bi-state region was threatened with severe thunderstorms on May 8th. Numerous supercells produced very large hail most of the day, and a small QLCS produced multiple tornadoes in southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Arcadia just west of the intersection of North Main Street and Elm Street. It tracked to the east through town snapping off several large trees. After crossing Russellville Road, it either uprooted or snapped off several more large trees on the Arcadia Valley Golf Club. The tornado then began to track more northeasterly and skirted the north shore of Lake Killarney snapping off even more trees as it crossed Highway 72 then crossed it once again, remaining on the north side of Highway 72 before crossing into Madison County. The tornado was rated EF1 in Iron County with a path length of 5.37 miles and a max path width of 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The bi-state region was threatened with severe thunderstorms on May 8th. Numerous supercells produced very large hail most of the day, and a small QLCS produced multiple tornadoes in southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado continued to track northeast along County Road 149. It snapped off or uprooted numerous trees. Also, a house sustained minor shingle damage to it���s roof. Then the tornado shifted it���s track a bit more easterly eventually crossing County Road 147, then Highway 49 around three quarters of a mile north of Vulcan.  As it crossed Highway F, 3.5 miles northeast of Vulcan, it uprooted several large trees. Then the tornado track shifted back to a northeasterly direction, crossing County Road 135, about 6 miles northeast of Vulcan, where it uprooted or snapped off several more large trees. Then the tornado crossed into southern Madison County. In Iron County, the path length was 12.05 miles with a max path width of 150 yards with a rating of EF1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex developed over Kansas during the early morning of August 13, 2024 and continued east towards southwest Missouri during the remainder of the morning. Despite the progressive nature of the system, heavy rainfall between 2 and 4.40 inches fell over Greene, Vernon, and Dade counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station FW7458 in Nevada measured 2.07 inches of storm total rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level shortwave energy pushed through the region during the early morning hours of August 14, 2024, sparking a band of convection over portions of southwest Missouri. As a result, heavy rain developed, with around 5 inches of rainfall causing areal and flash flooding in Wright, Howell, Douglas, and Ozark counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway H northeast of Tecumseh was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of light winds and clearing skies behind a cold frontal passage produced fog in portions of Southeast Missouri during the overnight hours, dissipating around sunrise as winds increased.

After a very dry March for the Current River, drier than average precipitation continued early April. This led to the introduction of D2 drought for part of Carter County April 9th, expanded to most of the county April 23rd. A rainy end to the month still left Carter below normal for April rainfall but, mild improvements in soil moisture, coupled with above average rainfall upstream on the Current River, provided enough relief to justify a change to D1 classification on the 30th.

Early morning, the surface low was located just north of St. Louis, Missouri, lifting to the NNE as it rapidly deepened. For Southeast Missouri, early morning effective bulk shear was around 60 kts, with a very high 25-30 kts of 0-1 km shear, while SRHs were around 200 m2/s2. MLCAPE was 1000-1500 J/kg, and mid-level lapse rates were 6.5 C/km. A 70-90 kt mid-level jet was west of the Quad State. A strong capping inversion suppressed storm activity in the evening, but the strong lift and upper level diffluence won out early morning. Two lines of storms developed after midnight in Southeast Missouri. The first had its southern end move through Perry County from 0100-0230CST. The latter was a very potent thunderstorm line racing eastward early morning April 2nd. Weaker thunderstorm activity occurred further south in the Ozark Foothills and near the bootheel. This line continued throughout the day all the way to West Virginia, being designated a derecho. For Southeast Missouri, 1 brief EF-0 tornado occurred west of Cape Girardeau, which transitioned into a microburst that progressed through residential areas in western Cape Girardeau city. Other instances of straight-line wind damage occurred in Cape Girardeau, Scott, and Stoddard Counties. According to county EMA, Cape Girardeau County had a total of 2 barns and 12 houses with reported damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm line moving eastward across northern Stoddard County produced a downburst that destroyed a barn and an irrigation system northeast of Bell City.

During the afternoon and evening of April 18, 2024, discrete supercell thunderstorms developed along a cold front moving through the bi-state area. These storms were initially capable of producing large hail and damaging wind gusts, but became tornadic as additional thunderstorms formed along the front. Due to the additional thunderstorms, storms congealed into a QLCS that impacted the St. Louis metro area. The QLCS was responsible for a tornado and straight line wind damage in this area. Fortunately, no injuries or fatalities were reported with these thunderstorms.

In the late evening hours of May 6th and early morning hours of May 7th, several severe linear features moved through Missouri from west to east. These produced widespread 60 mph straight line wind damage with numerous embedded QLCS mesovortices and a few confirmed QLCS tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed wires and power outages near Route N.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Missouri Ozarks on May 25, 2024, priming the atmosphere for moderate instability and sufficient deep layer shear. Additionally a low level jet around 45 to 50 knots nosed into the area during the late evening hours and into the early morning hours of May 26, 2024, further amplifying low level flow into the region. As a result, supercell thunderstorms developed and slid into the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours May 25 into May 26, 2024. These supercells produced hail between quarter and baseball size and flooding with rainfall totals between 3-4 inches. Widespread damaging winds were also observed, including a large swath of 70-80 mph winds in Barry county. Additionally, six tornadoes touched down across the Missouri Ozarks, producing damage ranging from EF-0 to EF-1 criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of hen egg size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms affected the Missouri Ozarks beginning on the evening of June 8th and continued through the morning of June 9th. Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred with both rounds. Significant flash flooding occurred overnight where 3-6 inches of rainfall occurred. Numerous water rescues occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down on Highway CC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds moved across southern Missouri during the afternoon of June 25th with additional storms later in the night into early on the morning of June 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed on A Highway south of Ava and northwest of Squires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind, large hail, and five tornadoes to the Missouri Ozarks from the evening of April 1st to the early morning hours of April 2nd. A tornado that occurred near Fair Grove was rated in EF-1. The four other tornadoes were rated EF-0 with three of them occurring from eastern Dallas County into Laclede County. The fourth EF-0 tornado occurred in St. James.||In addition to the tornadoes, storm surveys revealed several different swaths of damaging straight line wind speeds of 70-100 mph. Wind speeds of 90-100 mph caused roof damage to businesses in downtown Carthage with power poles and trees also snapped. Other swaths of significant wind damage occurred near Halltown, Elkland, and along the I-44 corridor from Waynesville to Rolla.||Several thunderstorms also produced hail from the size of quarters to tennis balls. Hail ranging from one to two inches in diameter occurred in an around Bolivar. Tennis ball size hail fell in the town of Maple Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of small creeks and streams and major flooding along the Marmaton and Little Osage River basins occurred thanks to heavy rainfall. From 2 to 4 inches of rainfall on already saturated ground from previous rainfall in the 25th. Isolated severe storms produced brief tornadoes in Vernon and St. Clair Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway O at the Spring River was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late evening hours of May 6th and early morning hours of May 7th, several severe linear features moved through Missouri from west to east. These produced widespread 60 mph straight line wind damage with numerous embedded QLCS mesovortices and a few confirmed QLCS tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of large trees downed and several small limbs near Route U between Route M and State Highway 13.
